original_abstract: In recent years, China has seen a wave of successful and largely peaceful protests against the production of para-xylene (PX), notably in cities like Xiamen, Dalian, and Ningbo. However, the 2014 anti-PX demonstration in Maoming stands as a significant and violent departure from this trend. Drawing on participant observation and in-depth interviews, this article analyzes the unique dynamics of the Maoming movement. The findings suggest that despite intensive government propaganda, local citizens possessed a sophisticated understanding of environmental risks, rooted in their long-term exposure to industrial pollution and awareness of previous anti-PX successes elsewhere. Unlike movements led by the urban middle class, the Maoming protest was characterized by a lack of formal organization and leadership, which contributed to its volatile nature and eventual suppression by the state. Furthermore, the study highlights how participants effectively utilized mobile social media and their intimate knowledge of the urban landscape to mobilize a large-scale uprising, even in the absence of support from mainstream media or established NGOs. This case study offers a critical perspective on the challenges and evolving tactics of grassroots environmentalism in contemporary China.
humanized_abstract: Over the past decade, China has witnessed a series of civic movements targeting the production of para-xylene (PX) successfully and peacefully in Xiamen, Dalian, and Ningbo. The violent anti-PX campaign in Maoming in 2014 undoubtedly broke the pattern. By combining ethnographic methods of participant observation and in-depth interviews, this paper looks into the special dynamics behind the Maoming campaign. Our fieldwork shows that despite the governmental campaigns promoting the PET industry, people in Maoming have already acquired, through their long-term experiences with local chemical plants and their extensive listening to and engaging with civic movements related to PX in other cities, the rudiments of environmental risk awareness. Different from the movements organized and participated by the urban middle class, Maoming did not have effective leaders or groups to control the violent activities, which made the Maoming anti-PX campaign especially unreasonable and hard to be contained by the state. Meanwhile, by drawing on examples from the use of social media in urban space during the campaigns, we argue that despite the governmental imposition of a ban on reports on the social media on the campaigns and the lack of coverage and participation of non-governmental organizations in the civic organizations can also use smart mobile devices to organize crowds to overthrow authorities in their own familiar home city. Finally, our case study also provides a critical look on the obstacles and ways of organizing environment-related social movements in contemporary China.
